title: Determining the Keyboard
text: Determining the Keyboard Uniface use the several mechanisms to determine which keyboard translation table to use for input. Operating system defaults—for example, on Unix the TERM environment variable indicates what kind of terminal is being used. If no other assignments are present, Uniface uses the default obtained from the operating system to determine the keyboard translation table used. Operating system symbols—if UKEYB is defined, it overrides the system default for the keybaord. If the devices used are applicable for everyone at your site, it is advisable to use operating system symbols to define the keyboard value. The definition should be placed in a system-wide logon script, if there is one. Assignment files—if the keyboard translation table you want to use is not in the default library (USYS), the $KEYBOARD and $VARIATION settings must be defined in the assignment file. It is good practice to set the library explicitly, however, to prevent someone else from inadvertently setting it to a library that does not contain the specified keyboard. If specific applications use different values, it is better to define them in a Uniface assignment file than rely on the other mechanisms. ProcScript statements—the $keyboard ProcScript function enables the user to switch between different keyboard layouts at different points in the application. If the change in tables is a transitory one, for example, from numeric to keypad, and back again, the change should be handled within the application using ProcScript. Setting UKEYB The UKEYB symbol overrides the system keyboard default. It has different names under each operating system and is set in different ways. For example: Under Windows, set the UKEYB environment variable. Under the Unix shell (sh) , use the following command to set the environment variable: UKEYB=vtnum;export UKEYB Copy Uppercase variables have priority over lowercase ones. Refer to the operating system documentation for details. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
